title: LLM Observability — Monitoring, Tracing & Evaluation at Scale
description: LLM observability is more complex than traditional application observability because LLMs are non-deterministic, their outputs are unstructured, and their failure modes are diverse (hallucination, bias, refusal, injection). At FAANG Staff+ level, you must design observability systems that track not just latency and error rates, but also output quality, safety compliance, cost attribution, and drift across model versions — at 1M+ inference requests per day.

sections.title: The Three Pillars of LLM Observability
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLM observability extends the standard three pillars (logs, metrics, traces) with LLM-specific concerns. Logs: every LLM interaction is logged — input, output, system prompt, tool calls, latency, model version, user ID. Metrics: traditional (latency p50/p95/p99, error rate, throughput) plus LLM-specific (hallucination rate, refusal rate, injection detection rate, cost per query, token count per query). Traces: distributed tracing across the LLM stack — guardrail checks, RAG retrieval, tool execution, streaming latency, post-processing. The Staff insight: the hardest part of LLM observability is output quality evaluation — you can't just check HTTP status codes. You need automated quality classifiers running on every response, or a representative sample.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Log volume: 1M requests/day at 2K tokens/request = ~2B tokens/day of log data (~10 GB/day compressed). Storage cost: ~$50/day for log storage, ~$200/day for indexed search (DataDog, Grafana). Trace sampling: 100% tracing for high-priority users, 1% for low-priority. Quality evaluation cost: automated LLM-as-judge evaluation costs ~$0.001-0.005 per response. At 1M requests/day, full evaluation costs $1K-5K/day. Solution: sample-based evaluation (10% of requests) + targeted full evaluation for anomalous cases. Alert fatigue: expect 1-2 actionable alerts per 100K requests. Noise reduction: use anomaly detection (statistical baselines) rather than static thresholds.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Instrument all LLM calls with a wrapper that captures: input, output, model, latency, token count, user ID, session ID, and request ID. 2) Export structured logs to your observability platform (JSON to stdout/DataDog/Grafana Loki). 3) Define dashboards: latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate, token usage, cost per model, cost per user. 4) Set up alerts: latency p99 > 5s, error rate > 5%, cost spike > 2x daily average. 5) Implement trace sampling: 100% for premium users, 1% for free tier. 6) Add quality evaluation: sample 10% of responses for automated evaluation (hallucination detection, safety check, helpfulness score). 7) Set up drift detection: compare weekly distributions of output length, sentiment, topic distribution.

search Interview Angle: "Design an observability system for an LLM-powered customer support chatbot handling 500K conversations/day." Key: structured logging, three-level quality eval (sample 100% of escalations, 10% of normal, 1% of automated), cost dashboards per team, drift detection on response sentiment/topic, anomaly alerting with dynamic baselines.
sections.codeBlock:
# LLM observability instrumentation wrapper
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from typing import Optional, Callable
import time, json, uuid, logging
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class LLMInteraction:
    request_id: str
    user_id: str
    session_id: str
    system_prompt: str
    user_input: str
    model_output: str
    model_name: str
    latency_ms: float
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    tool_calls: list[dict]
    guardrail_results: list[dict]
    error: Optional[str]
    timestamp: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.utcnow().isoformat())


class ObservableLLM:
    """
    Wrapper around an LLM client that logs every interaction.
    Exports structured logs for observability platform ingestion.
    """
    def __init__(self, llm_client, logger: logging.Logger = None):
        self.client = llm_client
        self.logger = logger or logging.getLogger("llm_observability")
        self.metrics = {
            "total_calls": 0, "total_tokens": 0,
            "total_latency_ms": 0, "errors": 0
        }

    async def complete(self, messages: list[dict], **kwargs) -> dict:
        """Make an observable LLM call."""
        request_id = uuid.uuid4().hex[:12]
        start = time.time()
        error = None
        output = ""
        tool_calls = []

        try:
            response = await self.client.chat.completions.create(
                messages=messages, **kwargs
            )
            latency = (time.time() - start) * 1000
            choice = response.choices[0]
            output = choice.message.content or ""
            if hasattr(choice.message, 'tool_calls'):
                tool_calls = [
                    {"name": tc.function.name,
                     "arguments": tc.function.arguments}
                    for tc in (choice.message.tool_calls or [])
                ]
            input_tokens = response.usage.prompt_tokens
            output_tokens = response.usage.completion_tokens
        except Exception as e:
            latency = (time.time() - start) * 1000
            error = str(e)
            input_tokens = kwargs.get("estimated_input_tokens", 0)
            output_tokens = 0

        # Record interaction
        interaction = LLMInteraction(
            request_id=request_id,
            user_id=kwargs.get("user_id", "anonymous"),
            session_id=kwargs.get("session_id", ""),
            system_prompt=kwargs.get("system", ""),
            user_input=messages[-1].get("content", "") if messages else "",
            model_output=output,
            model_name=kwargs.get("model", self.client.model or "unknown"),
            latency_ms=latency,
            input_tokens=input_tokens,
            output_tokens=output_tokens,
            tool_calls=tool_calls,
            guardrail_results=kwargs.get("guardrail_results", []),
            error=error
        )

        # Export
        self._export(interaction)
        self._update_metrics(interaction)

        return {
            "output": output,
            "tool_calls": tool_calls,
            "latency_ms": latency,
            "usage": {"input_tokens": input_tokens,
                      "output_tokens": output_tokens},
            "request_id": request_id
        }

    def _export(self, interaction: LLMInteraction):
        """Export interaction as structured log."""
        log_entry = {
            "type": "llm_interaction",
            "data": asdict(interaction)
        }
        self.logger.info(json.dumps(log_entry))

    def _update_metrics(self, interaction: LLMInteraction):
        self.metrics["total_calls"] += 1
        self.metrics["total_tokens"] += (
            interaction.input_tokens + interaction.output_tokens
        )
        self.metrics["total_latency_ms"] += interaction.latency_ms
        if interaction.error:
            self.metrics["errors"] += 1

    def get_metrics_snapshot(self) -> dict:
        m = self.metrics
        return {
            "total_calls": m["total_calls"],
            "total_tokens": m["total_tokens"],
            "avg_latency_ms": (m["total_latency_ms"]
                               / max(m["total_calls"], 1)),
            "error_rate": m["errors"] / max(m["total_calls"], 1)
        }

sections.title: Automated Quality Evaluation: LLM-as-Judge & Classifiers
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Traditional quality metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR) correlate poorly with human judgement for generative tasks. The industry standard is now LLM-as-Judge — using a strong LLM (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5) to evaluate the outputs of another LLM. The judge LLM is given a rubric and asked to score the response on dimensions like: helpfulness, accuracy, harmlessness, and verbosity. The Staff insight: LLM-as-Judge has known biases — it prefers longer responses, it agrees more with models similar to itself, and it's vulnerable to order effects (the order of presented options changes the score). Mitigations include: multi-dimensional rubrics, position randomisation, multiple judges, and calibration against human evaluation.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: LLM-as-Judge agreement with human evaluators: GPT-4 judge achieves 75-85% agreement (Cohen's kappa 0.6-0.7). A single LLM judge costs $0.001-0.01 per evaluation. Multi-judge ensemble (3 judges, majority vote): 85-90% agreement, cost $0.01-0.03 per evaluation. Rubric-based evaluation reduces bias by 15-20% compared to free-form scoring. Position bias: the first response in a pairwise comparison is preferred 55-65% of the time (randomise order to compensate). Classifier-based evaluation (BERT classifiers fine-tuned on human ratings): faster (10ms vs 1s) and cheaper ($0.0001 vs $0.01) but requires labelled data and re-training when the task changes. At FAANG scale, most production teams use a hybrid: classifier for real-time filtering + LLM-as-Judge for sampled deep evaluation.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Define evaluation dimensions: accuracy (factual correctness), helpfulness (solves the user's problem), safety (no harmful content), style (tone, length, format). 2) Create a rubric for each dimension with a 1-5 scale and anchor examples (what does a 1 look like? what does a 5 look like?). 3) Implement LLM-as-Judge: call the judge LLM with the rubric, the prompt, the response, and ask for a score per dimension. 4) Randomise presentation order in pairwise evaluations. 5) Use multiple judges (3-5) and take the majority or median score. 6) Calibrate against human evaluation: have 100 human-labelled examples, compute judge-human agreement, adjust rubric if agreement <80%. 7) Run quality evaluation on a sample of production traffic (10%) and log scores for dashboarding and alerting.

sections.title: Tracing: End-to-End Visibility Across the LLM Stack
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: An LLM request traverses many components: guardrails → LLM call → RAG retrieval → tool execution → output guardrails → post-processing. Without tracing, you can't tell which component caused a slow response, a hallucination, or a safety failure. Distributed tracing creates a single trace (a tree of spans) for each user request, capturing timing, inputs, outputs, and metadata at each step. The Staff insight: the LLM response itself is often the least informative part of the trace — the most valuable spans are the guardrails (did they fire correctly?), retrieval (was the right context retrieved?), and tool calls (were they executed correctly?).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Trace overhead: 0.1-0.5ms per span (minimal). Trace storage: ~1KB per span, ~10KB per trace. At 1M requests/day with 10 spans/request = 10GB/day of trace data. Sampling: trace 100% of requests with latency > 5s, 10% of normal requests, 1% of fast requests (adaptive sampling). Trace span breakdown for a typical RAG query: guardrail input (20ms) → LLM call (800ms) → retrieval (150ms) → tool calls (500ms) → guardrail output (20ms) = ~1.5s total. If the user reports a 5s response, the trace immediately shows which span is the bottleneck. Observability platforms: OpenTelemetry (open standard), LangSmith (LangChain), Weights & Biases Prompts, MLflow Tracing.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Instrument every component with OpenTelemetry spans: guardrail calls, LLM calls, retrieval calls, tool calls, post-processing. 2) Add trace context propagation: pass the trace ID through every component via headers or context variables. 3) Export spans to a trace backend (Jaeger, Grafana Tempo, DataDog APM). 4) Define service-level objectives (SLOs): e.g., 99% of traces complete in < 5s. 5) Build trace-based alerting: if a specific span type (e.g., tool execution) exceeds its p99 threshold, alert. 6) Use trace sampling to manage storage costs: head-based sampling (decide at request start) vs tail-based sampling (decide after completion based on attributes like latency, error status). 7) Correlate traces with logs and metrics via the trace ID for full debug context.
sections.codeBlock:
# OpenTelemetry tracing for LLM pipeline
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.trace import Status, StatusCode
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.http.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
import time, json

class LLMTracer:
    """
    OpenTelemetry-based tracer for LLM pipeline components.
    Creates spans for every step in the LLM request lifecycle.
    """
    def __init__(self, service_name: str = "llm-service"):
        provider = TracerProvider(
            resource=Resource.create({"service.name": service_name})
        )
        processor = BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter())
        provider.add_span_processor(processor)
        trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)
        self.tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)
        self.current_trace_id = None

    def create_trace(self, request_id: str) -> "Span":
        """Create a root span for a new request."""
        span = self.tracer.start_span(
            name="llm_request",
            attributes={
                "request_id": request_id,
                "service": "llm-service"
            }
        )
        self.current_trace_id = request_id
        return span

    def trace_guardrail(self, guardrail_name: str, input_data: str,
                        result: dict, latency_ms: float):
        """Trace a guardrail check."""
        with self.tracer.start_as_current_span(
            f"guardrail.{guardrail_name}"
        ) as span:
            span.set_attribute("guardrail.name", guardrail_name)
            span.set_attribute("guardrail.passed", str(result.get("passed", True)))
            span.set_attribute("guardrail.latency_ms", latency_ms)
            span.set_attribute("guardrail.score", result.get("score", 0.0))
            if not result.get("passed", True):
                span.set_status(Status(StatusCode.ERROR,
                     result.get("reason", "Guardrail blocked")))

    def trace_llm_call(self, model: str, prompt: str,
                       response: str, latency_ms: float,
                       input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int):
        """Trace an LLM inference call."""
        with self.tracer.start_as_current_span("llm.call") as span:
            span.set_attribute("llm.model", model)
            span.set_attribute("llm.input_tokens", input_tokens)
            span.set_attribute("llm.output_tokens", output_tokens)
            span.set_attribute("llm.latency_ms", latency_ms)
            span.set_attribute("llm.prompt_length", len(prompt))
            span.set_attribute("llm.response_length", len(response))

    def trace_retrieval(self, query: str, num_docs: int,
                        latency_ms: float, scores: list[float]):
        """Trace a RAG retrieval step."""
        with self.tracer.start_as_current_span("retrieval") as span:
            span.set_attribute("retrieval.query", query[:100])
            span.set_attribute("retrieval.num_docs", num_docs)
            span.set_attribute("retrieval.latency_ms", latency_ms)
            span.set_attribute("retrieval.top_score", max(scores) if scores else 0.0)
            span.set_attribute("retrieval.avg_score",
                               sum(scores)/len(scores) if scores else 0.0)

    def trace_tool_call(self, tool_name: str, arguments: dict,
                        result: str, latency_ms: float, success: bool):
        """Trace a tool execution call."""
        with self.tracer.start_as_current_span(f"tool.{tool_name}") as span:
            span.set_attribute("tool.name", tool_name)
            span.set_attribute("tool.latency_ms", latency_ms)
            span.set_attribute("tool.success", str(success))
            if not success:
                span.set_status(Status(StatusCode.ERROR, "Tool execution failed"))

    def end_trace(self, span, error: Optional[str] = None):
        """End the root trace span."""
        if error:
            span.set_status(Status(StatusCode.ERROR, error))
        else:
            span.set_status(Status(StatusCode.OK))
        span.end()

sections.title: Drift Detection: Model, Data & Behaviour Monitoring
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLM behaviour changes over time — model updates, prompt changes, retrieval pipeline changes, and shifts in user behaviour all cause drift. Model drift: the API model is updated silently (GPT-4o June 2025 vs GPT-4o March 2025) and behaves differently. Data drift: user query distribution changes (seasonal, new product, new feature). Behaviour drift: over time, the same prompt produces different responses (model non-determinism + temperature). Drift detection monitors these changes and alerts when they cross a threshold. The Staff insight: drift is inevitable. The goal is not to prevent drift but to detect it quickly, understand its impact, and respond — either by accepting it, updating the prompt, or rolling back the model version.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: GPT-4o minor version updates: output distribution changes measurably 3-5 times/year. Example: verbosity increased by 15% in one minor update. Embedding drift: sentence embedding models show distribution shift of 0.05-0.1 cosine similarity per year. Detection thresholds: alert if KL divergence > 0.1 between weekly and baseline distribution. Evaluation score drift: a 3% drop in automated quality score over a week is actionable. Sample size: detect a 5% shift with 95% confidence requires ~1000 samples per week. User satisfaction drift: CSAT score drop of 0.2 on a 5-point scale over a month triggers investigation. Prompt effectiveness: 10-20% of prompts need revision every quarter due to model behaviour shifts.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Establish baselines: run 1000 test queries weekly, log outputs, compute distribution of output length, sentiment, topic, quality scores. 2) Set up weekly comparison: compare this week's distribution to the baseline using statistical tests (KS test, KL divergence). 3) Track quality score drift: compute mean quality score per week, alert on 3% drop. 4) Track embedding drift: embed all user queries weekly, compute centroid shift from baseline. 5) Track model version in logs: when a model provider updates their model, the version string changes — alert on version change. 6) Monitor user feedback: track thumbs up/down rate, CSAT score, escalation rate. 7) Build drift dashboards: show weekly trends for all metrics with alerting thresholds. 8) When drift is detected, investigate: did the model update? Did the prompt change? Did user behaviour change? Apply corrective action (rollback, prompt update, re-evaluation).

sections.title: Cost Observability: Attribution, Budgeting & Optimisation
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLM costs scale with usage — more users, longer contexts, more tool calls, higher-quality models. Without cost observability, costs can silently grow 10x in a month. Cost attribution tracks spending per: user, team, feature, model, and prompt type. Budgeting sets limits per dimension and alerts when exceeded. Optimisation uses cost data to choose the cheapest model that meets quality requirements. The Staff insight: cost observability is the single most under-invested area in LLM production systems. Most teams deploy to production without understanding their cost structure, leading to bill shock when usage grows. Implement cost tracking from day 1 — retroactively adding it is painful.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Cost structure: GPT-4o input $2.50/1M tokens, output $10/1M tokens. GPT-4o-mini input $0.15/1M, output $0.60/1M. Ratio: GPT-4o is ~16x more expensive than GPT-4o-mini. Average query cost: $0.005-0.05 for GPT-4o-mini, $0.05-0.50 for GPT-4o. Budget allocation: 60% to LLM inference, 20% to embeddings/retrieval, 10% to guardrails, 10% to evaluation. Cost per user: $0.10-1.00/user/month for light usage, $10-100/user/month for heavy usage. Cost alerts: set at 80% and 100% of monthly budget. Tracking granularity: per-model cost per day, per-user cost per week, per-feature cost per month. Cost optimisation: model routing saves 30-50% (simple queries use GPT-4o-mini, complex ones use GPT-4o). Caching saves 20-40% for repeated queries.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Tag every LLM call with cost-attribution metadata: user_id, team, feature, model, prompt template ID. 2) Log token counts per call (input + output) and compute cost using provider pricing. 3) Build cost dashboards: cost per model per day, cost per user per week, cost per feature per month. 4) Set monthly budgets per team/feature with hard limits (e.g., feature X cannot exceed $10K/month). 5) Implement cost-based routing: classify each query by complexity, route simple queries to cheaper models. 6) Implement prompt caching: cache responses for identical prompts (common in automated systems). 7) Monitor cost anomalies: if a user's cost spikes 2x in a day, investigate. 8) Report cost efficiency: cost per resolved ticket, cost per conversation, cost per generated response.

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — LLM Observability
sections.description:
search Q1 (Google L6): "Design an observability system for a multi-model LLM platform serving 100+ internal teams." A: Centralised observability platform with per-team data isolation. Core components: (1) Unified logging: every LLM call emits structured JSON with team ID, model, prompt template, latency, tokens. (2) Per-team dashboards: teams see only their own data, plus anonymised aggregate benchmarks. (3) Centralised quality evaluation: a shared LLM-as-Judge service runs weekly evaluations on sampled data from each team. (4) Cost attribution: each team has a monthly budget, tracked and alerted via the cost dashboard. (5) Model version tracking: centralised registry of model versions with changelog and deprecation notices. (6) Anomaly detection: global models detect cross-team anomalies (e.g., all teams experiencing higher latency indicates a platform issue). (7) Self-serve: teams can create custom alerts and dashboards without platform team involvement.

search Q2 (Meta E6): "A user reports that the chatbot gave them incorrect information. How do you debug using observability data?" A: Step 1: find the trace for the user's session using their user_id and timestamp. Step 2: examine the LLM response quality score from the automated evaluator. Step 3: check the guardrail trace — did any guardrail fire? Should it have caught this? Step 4: examine the RAG retrieval trace — was the correct document retrieved? What was the similarity score? Step 5: check if this is an isolated incident or part of a pattern (search for similar responses in the last 24 hours). Step 6: if it's a pattern, check drift detection — did the model or retrieval pipeline change recently? Step 7: add this specific example to the regression test suite and update the evaluation rubric to catch similar errors.

search Q3 (Anthropic L5): "What metrics would you track for a streaming LLM application (token-by-token output)?" A: Standard metrics: latency (time to first token, time to last token, tokens per second). Streaming-specific: time-to-first-token p95 (should be <500ms), inter-token latency (jitter — variance in time between tokens), streaming throughput (tokens/second), backpressure events (how often is the client too slow to consume?). Quality metrics: same as non-streaming (evaluation on complete response). User experience: how many users disconnect before the response completes? (Abandonment rate). Cost: streaming doesn't change token cost but may increase latency overhead. Trace: instrument the streaming connection lifecycle — connection establishment, first token, stream completion/error.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• OpenTelemetry LLM Semantic Conventions — Standardised tracing conventions for LLM applications (prompts, completions, embeddings, retrieval).
  • LangSmith: LLM Observability Platform — Tracing, evaluation, and monitoring for LangChain-based applications.
  • Weights & Biases Prompts — LLM observability and prompt engineering platform with trace visualisation.
  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena (Zheng et al., 2023) — Analysis of LLM judge biases and calibration methods.
  • Evidently: LLM Evaluation & Drift Detection — Open-source library for LLM output quality and drift monitoring.
  • LLM Evaluation: A Comprehensive Survey (Chang et al., 2024) — Survey of evaluation methods, metrics, and best practices.

details:
LLM Observability Decision Flow: Start with structured logging (capture every LLM interaction). Instrument with OpenTelemetry tracing for end-to-end visibility. Add basic metrics (latency, error rate, token usage). Implement cost attribution (per user, team, model, feature). Add LLM-as-Judge quality evaluation on a sampled basis. Set up drift detection (weekly distribution comparisons). Build dashboards for each stakeholder category (engineering, product, finance). Configure alerts for anomalies. At FAANG scale, observability is not a tool — it's a discipline that spans logging, metrics, tracing, evaluation, cost tracking, and drift monitoring. Invest in the observability platform before you need it.

Related: LLM Security & Guardrails | Tool Use & Function Calling | Synthetic Data Generation
